statement: Iceland's fisheries catch has remained broadly stable over the 2020–2023 period, ranging from 1.43 million tonnes (2020) to 1.97 million tonnes (2022). By contrast, the EU-27's combined catch appears to have declined from 8.9 million tonnes in 2020 to around 2.4 million tonnes in 2023, though 2023 data may be incomplete. Among the smaller Nordic EU members, Sweden's catch declined from 2.4 million tonnes (2020) to 2.0 million tonnes (2023), and Finland's from 1.7 million tonnes to 1.4 million tonnes over the same period.
caveats: The apparent sharp decline in EU-27 catch after 2022 likely reflects incomplete data reporting rather than actual catch reduction. Fisheries data is subject to significant revision. Species-level data would provide more meaningful comparisons than raw tonnage.

related_entries.statement: Iceland is one of Europe's largest fishing nations despite its small population. In 2023, Iceland's total fisheries catch was approximately 1.92 million tonnes, compared to Norway's 30.6 million tonnes, Denmark's 6.9 million tonnes, and the entire EU-27's combined catch. On a per capita basis, Iceland's catch of roughly 5,050 kg per person dwarfs all comparators: Norway at 5,660 kg/person, Denmark at 1,170 kg/person, and the EU-27 average at well under 100 kg/person. The 2024 data (partial) shows Iceland at 1.33 million tonnes, likely reflecting incomplete reporting.

Fishing and fish processing together contributed approximately 8% of Iceland's GDP in 2024, down from roughly 12% in 2005 and over 16% in 1990. Fishing alone (excluding processing) accounted for about 4.5% of GDP. The sector's relative economic importance has declined steadily as the economy has diversified.
